question: Which under-served Agent Skill project should be built for broad GitHub adoption, and how should Skill Vaccine be developed from the relevant research?

notes: Used public web search, arXiv PDFs, local paper-summary-agent extraction scripts, role-scoped analysis, and RDD TODO conversion.
This trace was added after audit because the initial run preserved prose synthesis but not complete machine-readable lineage.

corpus_catalog.description: Hierarchical triage framework for detecting malicious AI agent skills using static checks, structured semantic decomposition, and LLM jury review.
Permission framework for Agent Skills with manifests, deny-by-default runtime access control, capability inference, and monitoring.
Marketplace auditing framework for semantic-behavioral alignment and cross-skill combinatorial risk simulation.
SKILL.md-only semantic supply-chain attacks against discovery, selection, and governance stages of skill registries.
Package manager and registry for Agent Skill packages, including conformance diagnostics and skillsets.
Dynamic evaluation framework for open skill quality, skill usage, task adherence, and cost-performance tradeoffs.
Benchmark for measuring whether Agent Skills improve task performance through paired no-skill and skill-augmented evaluation.
Data-driven analysis of the Agent Skill ecosystem, including growth, category concentration, adoption, and supply-demand imbalance.

required_facts.description: Evidence that Agent Skill security is a large and underserved problem.
Detection architecture suitable for Skill Vaccine MVP and later high-recall semantic review.
Permission/capability governance requirements for skill artifacts.
Registry, conformance, and evaluation features that improve adoption and trust.
Known limitations and follow-up work that should become RDD TODOs.

routes.reason: Ecosystem scale, semantic supply-chain attacks, and marketplace audit findings establish demand and risk.
SkillSieve defines layered detection; SKILL.md attack paper defines registry-facing semantic manipulation.
SkillGuard defines permission artifacts and runtime governance; SkillProbe defines alignment and risk graph needs.
These corpora cover package conformance, skill quality evaluation, and benchmark-driven usefulness.
Limitations are distributed across detection, permissions, composition, and evaluation papers.

stop_conditions: Each must-have fact has at least two independent supporting snippets when possible.
Unsupported claims become TODOs or are removed from final synthesis.
Stop when the RDD TODO set covers every high-risk missing implementation area.

subqueries.query: SkillSieve Layer 1 Layer 2 Layer 3 malicious agent skills F1 cost
subqueries.reason: Recover detection pipeline, metrics, and implementation-relevant signals.

subqueries.query: SkillGuard permission manifest deny-by-default capability inference taxonomy F1
subqueries.reason: Recover permission model and manifest-generation requirements.

subqueries.query: SkillProbe semantic behavioral alignment cross skill risk graph marketplace audit
subqueries.reason: Recover cross-skill composition and admission findings.

subqueries.query: SKILL.md semantic supply chain discovery selection governance evasion Top-10 77.6
subqueries.reason: Recover SKILL.md-only attack classes that static code scanners miss.

subqueries.query: Skilldex conformance score line-level diagnostics Agent Skill registry metadata
OpenSkillEval SkillsBench skill read rate pass rate normalized gain evaluation hooks

retrieved_snippets.evidence_summary: SkillSieve uses Layer 1 static checks, Layer 2 structured semantic decomposition, and Layer 3 LLM jury; reports strong final F1 and low per-skill cost.
SkillGuard treats skills as permission-bearing executable artifacts and combines manifests, runtime access control, user authorization, deny-by-default, capability inference, and monitoring.
SkillProbe audits admission, semantic-behavioral alignment, and cross-skill combinatorial risk; marketplace popularity is not a reliable security proxy.
SKILL.md-only changes can manipulate discovery, selection, and governance even without changing executable code.
Skilldex motivates package-manager and registry-level conformance scoring with line-level diagnostics and metadata.
OpenSkillEval shows skill availability does not guarantee effective use and motivates dynamic task-grounded evaluation hooks.
SkillsBench motivates paired no-skill versus skill-augmented evaluation with deterministic verifiers.
The agent skill ecosystem is large, rapidly changing, and imbalanced across categories, supporting demand for tooling.

draft_answer.summary: Skill Vaccine is justified as a local-first Agent Skill security and governance scanner. The MVP should start with static Layer 1 scanning, structured outputs, and permission inference, while preserving TODOs for semantic review, jury review, manifest policy, cross-skill risk graphs, conformance, and evaluation hooks.

missing_facts: Line-precise citations in the final research synthesis are still incomplete for every claim.
Official artifact/dataset schemas from the papers have not been vendored or normalized.

feedback_queries.query: paper artifact dataset schema SkillSieve SkillGuard SkillProbe GitHub repository
feedback_queries.reason: Needed before benchmark fixture suite or artifact-compatible evaluation.

context_assessment.reason: Enough evidence exists for product architecture and RDD TODO planning, but not for final benchmark implementation.

iterations.notes: Initial web search identified candidate papers and adjacent tools. This was acceptable because corpus candidates were not known yet.
Papers were fetched, extracted, and assigned to role-scoped analysis: method/security, permission/governance, ecosystem/evaluation.
Subagent findings and retrieved evidence were converted into RDD TODOs and MVP implementation decisions.
This file and the compliance audit were added to preserve Google-style query lineage, snippet provenance, sufficiency assessment, and grounded synthesis mapping.

grounded_answer.answer: Skill Vaccine should be developed as a local-first Agent Skill scanner with static Layer 1 detection, structured JSON/SARIF output, capability inference, manifest suggestions, and a staged roadmap for semantic review, jury review, permission governance, cross-skill risk graphs, conformance, and evaluation.

citations.claim: The MVP should start with static Layer 1 detection and later add semantic and jury layers.
Permission/capability inference and manifest suggestions are core to governance.
Conformance, registry metadata, and evaluation hooks are needed for adoption and trust.
Cross-skill risk and SKILL.md-only manipulation must remain explicit follow-up work.

missing_facts: Paper artifact schema normalization remains future work.
Individual Korean paper summaries remain future work.
